Redox reactions involving the transfer of hydrogen atoms are essentially the underpinning basis for exercise metabolism during endurance-type exercise, because it is the transfer of hydrogen atoms from the food we eat to oxygen molecules (to produce water) which produces the energy store (ATP) to fuel our muscles during exercise. In such circumstances, oxygen is said to be the final *electron acceptor*, as it undergoes a four-electron reduction (by accepting four hydrogen atoms) to produce water. Collectively, this process is known as *oxidative phosphorylation* and we will cover this in detail in Chapter 5.

Oxidation reactions are usually exergonic reactions as they involve the loss of hydrogen atoms from energy-rich reduced compounds (containing many hydrogen atoms) to lower energy compounds. In the case of oxidation of food such as carbohydrates, some of the energy released through the breakage of bonds (i.e. removal of hydrogen atoms) is used to phosphorylate adenosine diphosphate (ADP) to ATP (i.e. *substrate phosphorylation*). The energy has therefore been transferred and stored in ATP molecules so that it can later be used to drive energy-consuming processes such as exercise.

## Definition

Redox reactions involving the transfer of hydrogen atoms form the basis of exercise metabolism during endurance-type exercise, whereby hydrogen atoms from food are transferred to oxygen molecules to produce water and generate ATP to fuel muscular work.

## Core Principles

- Oxidation reactions are typically exergonic, involving the loss of hydrogen atoms from energy-rich reduced compounds (containing many hydrogen atoms) to lower energy compounds.
- In endurance metabolism, oxygen acts as the final *electron acceptor*, undergoing a four-electron reduction (by accepting four hydrogen atoms) to form water.
- The coupled transfer of hydrogen atoms to oxygen for ATP synthesis is collectively termed *oxidative phosphorylation*.
- During oxidation of carbohydrates and other fuels, some of the energy released by bond breakage (removal of hydrogen atoms) is used to phosphorylate ADP to ATP via *substrate phosphorylation*.
- Energy released by oxidation is transferred and stored in ATP molecules for later use in driving energy-consuming processes such as muscular contraction during exercise.

## Sub-concepts

- *Electron acceptor*: in aerobic metabolism, oxygen serves as the terminal electron acceptor, accepting four electrons (with four hydrogen atoms) to produce water.
- *Oxidative phosphorylation*: the overall process by which hydrogen-atom transfer to oxygen drives ATP synthesis.
- *Substrate phosphorylation*: ATP synthesis through direct phosphorylation of ADP using energy released from breaking substrate bonds during fuel oxidation.

## Practical Applications

- Endurance exercise relies on the oxidation of food substrates (e.g. carbohydrates) to supply ATP for muscular work, with ATP acting as the energy currency that powers contraction.
- Understanding redox-driven ATP production underpins interpretation of fuel use, oxygen consumption, and metabolic capacity during sustained physical activity.
